content:
# Genesis Chapter 14 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 8r-8v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_014_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_014_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 9-10 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0013

---

*The War of the Kings (vv. 1–12)*

**14:1** And it was [וַיְהִי] in the days of [בִּימֵי] Amraphel king of Shinar [אַמְרָפֶל מֶלֶךְ-שִׁנְעָר] Arioch king of Ellasar [אַרְיוֹךְ מֶלֶךְ אֶלָּסָר] Chedorlaomer king of Elam [כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר מֶלֶךְ עֵילָם] and Tidal king of Goiim [וְתִדְעָל מֶלֶךְ גּוֹיִם].

**14:2** They made war [עָשׂוּ מִלְחָמָה] with Bera king of Sodom [אֶת-בֶּרַע מֶלֶךְ סְדֹם] and with Birsha king of Gomorrah [וְאֶת-בִּרְשַׁע מֶלֶךְ עֲמֹרָה] Shinab king of Admah [שִׁנְאָב מֶלֶךְ אַדְמָה] and Shemeber king of Zeboiim [וְשֶׁמְאֵבֶר מֶלֶךְ צְבֹיִים] and the king of Bela [וּמֶלֶךְ בֶּלַע] — that is Zoar [הִיא-צֹעַר].

**14:3** All these [כָּל-אֵלֶּה] joined together [חָבְרוּ] to the Valley of Siddim [אֶל-עֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים] — that is the Salt Sea [הוּא יָם הַמֶּלַח].

**14:4** Twelve years [שְׁתֵּים עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה] they had served [עָבְדוּ] Chedorlaomer [אֶת-כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר] and in the thirteenth year [וּשְׁלֹשׁ-עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה] they rebelled [מָרָדוּ].

**14:5** And in the fourteenth year [וּבְאַרְבַּע עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה] Chedorlaomer came [בָּא כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר] and the kings that were with him [וְהַמְּלָכִים אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ] and they struck [וַיַּכּוּ] the Rephaim in Ashteroth-karnaim [אֶת-רְפָאִים בְּעַשְׁתְּרֹת קַרְנַיִם] and the Zuzim in Ham [וְאֶת-הַזּוּזִים בְּהָם] and the Emim in Shaveh-kiriathaim [וְאֵת הָאֵמִים בְּשָׁוֵה קִרְיָתָיִם].

**14:6** And the Horites in their hill-country of Seir [וְאֶת-הַחֹרִי בְּהַרְרָם שֵׂעִיר] as far as El-paran [עַד-אֵיל פָּארָן] which is by the wilderness [אֲשֶׁר עַל-הַמִּדְבָּר].

**14:7** And they turned back [וַיָּשֻׁבוּ] and came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] to En-mishpat [אֶל-עֵין מִשְׁפָּט] — that is Kadesh [הִוא קָדֵשׁ] — and they struck [וַיַּכּוּ] all the field of the Amalekites [אֶת-כָּל-שְׂדֵה הָעֲמָלֵקִי] and also the Amorites [וְגַם אֶת-הָאֱמֹרִי] who were dwelling in Hazazon-tamar [הַיֹּשֵׁב בְּחַצְצֹן תָּמָר].

**14:8** And the king of Sodom went out [וַיֵּצֵא מֶלֶךְ-סְדֹם] and the king of Gomorrah [וּמֶלֶךְ עֲמֹרָה] and the king of Admah [וּמֶלֶךְ אַדְמָה] and the king of Zeboiim [וּמֶלֶךְ צְבֹיִים] and the king of Bela [וּמֶלֶךְ בֶּלַע] — that is Zoar [הִוא-צֹעַר] — and they drew up in battle [וַיַּעַרְכוּ אִתָּם מִלְחָמָה] against them in the Valley of Siddim [בְּעֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים].

**14:9** Against Chedorlaomer king of Elam [אֵת כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר מֶלֶךְ עֵילָם] and Tidal king of Goiim [וְתִדְעָל מֶלֶךְ גּוֹיִם] and Amraphel king of Shinar [וְאַמְרָפֶל מֶלֶךְ שִׁנְעָר] and Arioch king of Ellasar [וְאַרְיוֹךְ מֶלֶךְ אֶלָּסָר] — four kings against the five [אַרְבָּעָה מְלָכִים אֶת-הַחֲמִשָּׁה].

**14:10** And the Valley of Siddim [וְעֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים] was full of tar pits [בֶּאֱרֹת בֶּאֱרֹת חֵמָר] and the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled [וַיָּנֻסוּ מֶלֶךְ-סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה] and fell there [וַיִּפְּלוּ-שָׁמָּה] and the remnant [וְהַנִּשְׁאָרִים] fled to the hill-country [הֶהָרָה נָּסוּ].

**14:11** And they took [וַיִּקְחוּ] all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah [אֶת-כָּל-רְכֻשׁ סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה] and all their food [וְאֶת-כָּל-אָכְלָם] and they went [וַיֵּלֵכוּ].

**14:12** And they took [וַיִּקְחוּ] Lot [אֶת-לוֹט] and his goods [וְאֶת-רְכֻשׁוֹ] — the son of Abram's brother [בֶּן-אֲחִי אַבְרָם] — and they went [וַיֵּלֵכוּ] for he was dwelling in Sodom [וְהוּא יֹשֵׁב בִּסְדֹם].

---

*Abram Rescues Lot (vv. 13–16)*

**14:13** And a fugitive came [וַיָּבֹא הַפָּלִיט] and told [וַיַּגֵּד] Abram the Hebrew [לְאַבְרָם הָעִבְרִי] — and he was dwelling [וְהוּא שֹׁכֵן] by the oaks of Mamre [בְּאֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא] the Amorite [הָאֱמֹרִי] brother of Eshcol [אֲחִי אֶשְׁכֹּל] and brother of Aner [וַאֲחִי עָנֵר] and they [were] Abram's covenant-allies [וְהֵם בַּעֲלֵי בְרִית-אַבְרָם].

**14:14** And Abram heard [וַיִּשְׁמַע אַבְרָם] that his kinsman had been taken captive [כִּי נִשְׁבָּה אָחִיו] and he mustered [וַיָּרֶק] his trained men [אֶת-חֲנִיכָיו] the born-ones of his house [יְלִידֵי בֵיתוֹ] three hundred and eighteen [שְׁמֹנָה עָשָׂר וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת] and he pursued [וַיִּרְדֹּף] as far as Dan [עַד-דָּן].

**14:15** And he divided against them [וַיֵּחָלֵק עֲלֵיהֶם] by night [לַיְלָה] he and his servants [הוּא וַעֲבָדָיו] and he struck them [וַיַּכֵּם] and pursued them [וַיִּרְדְּפֵם] as far as Hobah [עַד-חוֹבָה] which is to the north of Damascus [אֲשֶׁר מִשְּׂמֹאל לְדַמָּשֶׂק].

**14:16** And he brought back [וַיָּשֶׁב] all the goods [אֶת-כָּל-הָרְכֻשׁ] and also Lot his kinsman [וְגַם אֶת-לוֹט אָחִיו] and his goods he brought back [וּרְכֻשׁוֹ הֵשִׁיב] and also the women [וְגַם אֶת-הַנָּשִׁים] and the people [וְאֶת-הָעָם].

---

*Melchizedek Blesses Abram (vv. 17–20)*

**14:17** And the king of Sodom went out [וַיֵּצֵא מֶלֶךְ-סְדֹם] to meet him [לִקְרָאתוֹ] after his return [אַחֲרֵי שׁוּבוֹ] from striking Chedorlaomer [מֵהַכּוֹת אֶת-כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר] and the kings that were with him [וְאֶת-הַמְּלָכִים אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ] to the Valley of Shaveh [אֶל-עֵמֶק שָׁוֵה] — that is the Valley of the King [הוּא עֵמֶק הַמֶּלֶךְ].

**14:18** And Melchizedek king of Salem [וּמַלְכִּי-צֶדֶק מֶלֶךְ שָׁלֵם] brought out [הוֹצִיא] bread and wine [לֶחֶם וָיָיִן] — and he [was] a priest [וְהוּא כֹהֵן] to God Most High [לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן].

**14:19** And he blessed him [וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ] and said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Blessed be Abram [בָּרוּךְ אַבְרָם] to God Most High [לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן] possessor of heaven and earth [קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ].

**14:20** And blessed be God Most High [וּבָרוּךְ אֵל עֶלְיוֹן] who has delivered [אֲשֶׁר-מִגֵּן] your enemies [צָרֶיךָ] into your hand [בְּיָדֶךָ] and he gave him a tithe [וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ מַעֲשֵׂר] of all [מִכֹּל].

---

*Abram's Refusal of the King of Sodom (vv. 21–24)*

**14:21** And the king of Sodom said [וַיֹּאמֶר מֶלֶךְ-סְדֹם] to Abram [אֶל-אַבְרָם]: Give me [תֶּן-לִי] the persons [הַנֶּפֶשׁ] and the goods take for yourself [וְהָרְכֻשׁ קַח-לָךְ].

**14:22** And Abram said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָם] to the king of Sodom [אֶל-מֶלֶךְ סְדֹם]: I lift up my hand [הֲרִמֹתִי יָדִי] to the LORD God Most High [אֶל-יְהוָה אֵל עֶלְיוֹן] possessor of heaven and earth [קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ].

**14:23** If [אִם] from a thread [מֵחוּט] to a sandal-thong [וְעַד שְׂרוֹךְ-נַעַל] and if I take [וְאִם-אֶקַּח] anything [מִכָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לָךְ] that is yours — so that you will not say [וְלֹא תֹאמַר] I have made Abram rich [אֲנִי הֶעֱשַׁרְתִּי אֶת-אַבְרָם].

**14:24** Except only [בִּלְעָדַי רַק] what the young men have eaten [אֲשֶׁר אָכְלוּ הַנְּעָרִים] and the portion of the men [וְחֵלֶק הָאֲנָשִׁים] who went with me [אֲשֶׁר הָלְכוּ אִתִּי] — Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre [עָנֵר אֶשְׁכֹּל וּמַמְרֵא] — they shall take their portion [הֵם יִקְחוּ חֶלְקָם].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 14:1 — The Four Kings: Political Geography of the Ancient Near East

**וַיְהִי בִּימֵי אַמְרָפֶל מֶלֶךְ-שִׁנְעָר** — "And it was in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar."

The opening formula **וַיְהִי בִּימֵי** ("and it was in the days of") is the standard biblical formula for situating a narrative within a particular era, typically one of high historical consequence (compare Judges 1:1; Ruth 1:1; Isaiah 1:1; Jeremiah 1:2). It gives Genesis 14 the character of historical chronicle rather than patriarchal family narrative — this chapter stands alone in its political, military, and diplomatic register.

**The four attacking kings:**
- **Amraphel king of Shinar** (שִׁנְעָר = Mesopotamia/Babylonia; associated with Babel in 10:10 and 11:2)
- **Arioch king of Ellasar** (Ellasar: debated; possibly Larsa in southern Mesopotamia)
- **Chedorlaomer king of Elam** (Elam: a major power east of Mesopotamia, modern southwest Iran; he is the dominant figure — the five kings had served him for twelve years, v. 4)
- **Tidal king of Goiim** (Goiim: גּוֹיִם, simply "nations/peoples"; Tidal has been tentatively linked to Hittite-sphere rulers; the name appears in Hittite records as Tudhaliya)

**Amraphel and Hammurabi:** An older scholarly hypothesis identified Amraphel with the famous Hammurabi of Babylon. This identification is now largely abandoned on linguistic and chronological grounds. The names do not correspond well, and the historical context does not align cleanly. The EMET does not endorse this identification.

**The historicity question:** Genesis 14 has long puzzled scholars — it reads like a historical archive document embedded within the patriarchal narrative, yet no external source corroborates the specific coalition or campaign. The names have partial ANE parallels but are not verified as a unit. The EMET translates the text as received; the question of historical verifiability lies outside the scope of translation.

---

### On 14:3 — עֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים: The Valley of Siddim / Salt Sea

**הוּא יָם הַמֶּלַח** — "that is the Salt Sea."

The narrator's gloss identifies the Valley of Siddim with the Salt Sea (יָם הַמֶּלַח, the Dead Sea). This is an **etiological note** — explaining to the reader that the site of this ancient battle is now underwater. The Dead Sea (the lowest body of water on earth, approximately 430 meters below sea level) was understood in Israelite tradition to cover the plain of Sodom and Gomorrah, submerged as a consequence of the divine destruction of Genesis 19. The narrator writes from after that destruction, when the valley is already the salt sea.

**בֶּאֱרֹת בֶּאֱרֹת חֵמָר (v. 10):** "tar pits, tar pits" — the doubled noun is a distributive plural, indicating the valley was scattered with bitumen/tar pits. The Dead Sea region (especially the southeast shore) has yielded evidence of natural asphalt deposits in antiquity. The tar pits become a battlefield trap for the fleeing kings.

---

### On 14:13 — הָעִבְרִי: Abram the Hebrew

**לְאַבְרָם הָעִבְרִי** — "to Abram the Hebrew."

This is the **first occurrence of the term עִבְרִי ('Ivri, "Hebrew")** in the Bible. It is applied to Abram — and it is applied to him by an outsider, in a narrative context where he is operating among Canaanite and Amorite peoples. The term functions as an ethnic or social marker distinguishing Abram from his surroundings.

**Etymology and meaning of עִבְרִי:**

The derivation of עִבְרִי is genuinely uncertain. The major proposals are:

1. **From עֵבֶר (Ever/Eber):** Abram's ancestor in the line of Shem (10:21-25; 11:14-17). "Hebrew" = descendant of Eber. This is the most common traditional identification. The LXX at 10:21 calls Shem "the father of all the sons of Eber" (πάντων τῶν υἱῶν Εβερ), treating Eber as a significant ancestor.

2. **From עָבַר ("to cross over"):** "Hebrew" = "one who crosses over" — one who has crossed the Euphrates (Abram coming from Mesopotamia) or more broadly a wanderer/migrant. This yields the sense "crosser, traveler" — a social description rather than a genealogical one.

3. **Linguistic connection to 'apiru/habiru:** A class of people attested widely across ANE documents (Egyptian, Ugaritic, Amarna letters) as **'apiru** — a marginalized, rootless, or mercenary social class. The connection between עִבְרִי ('ivri) and 'apiru (habiru) is phonologically possible but debated. If valid, "Hebrew" would originally have been a social designation (landless, migrant, mercenary class) before becoming an ethnic one.

**The EMET renders "the Hebrew" as the most direct literal equivalent.** No gloss can capture the full semantic range of the term at its first occurrence. What is clear is that *from this moment*, the word עִבְרִי is attached to the Abrahamic line and will become the standard ethnic identifier for the people of Israel in narratives where foreigners name or address them (Joseph called "a Hebrew young man," Gen 39:14; Israelites called "Hebrews" by Egyptians and Philistines).

**Mamre the Amorite, brother of Eshcol and Aner:** These three Amorite brothers appear as Abram's **בַּעֲלֵי בְרִית** ("covenant-allies," literally "lords/masters of covenant" or "covenant-partners"). This is the only occurrence of this precise phrase in the patriarchal narratives; it marks a formal alliance relationship. Mamre's oak/terebinth grove is Abram's dwelling place (13:18); these are his local neighbors and partners. They participate in the rescue (v. 24: they receive their portion of the spoil).

---

### On 14:14 — חֲנִיכִים: "Trained Men" / "Retainers"

**וַיָּרֶק אֶת-חֲנִיכָיו יְלִידֵי בֵיתוֹ שְׁמֹנָה עָשָׂר וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת** — "and he mustered his trained men, the born-ones of his house, three hundred and eighteen."

**חֲנִיכָיו (chanichav):** The noun **חֲנִיךְ** is a **hapax legomenon** — it appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. Its meaning must be deduced from context and cognates. The root **חָנַךְ (khanak)** means "to initiate, dedicate, train" — the same root gives us **חֲנֻכָּה** (Hanukkah, "dedication"). The **חֲנִיכִים** are therefore men who have been "dedicated" or "trained" — retainers brought up from birth within Abram's household for service, including military service.

The phrase **יְלִידֵי בֵיתוֹ** ("born-ones of his house") modifies or specifies the חֲנִיכִים — these are not hired mercenaries or recently acquired servants but men born into Abram's household, raised from infancy in his service. This constitutes a kind of private standing army — the armed force of a wealthy pastoralist-chieftain in the patriarchal period.

**The number 318:** The specificity of **שְׁמֹנָה עָשָׂר וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת** (318) is striking. Several interpretive traditions have treated it as a coded or symbolic number:
- Ancient gematria: the Hebrew letters for **אֱלִיעֶזֶר** (Eliezer, Abram's servant, Gen. 15:2) sum to 318 (א=1, ל=30, י=10, ע=70, ז=7, ר=200 = 318). This interpretation appears in the Epistle of Barnabas (c. 2nd century CE, ch. 9) — Barnabas argues that the 318 is a Christological cipher: the Greek letters for "IH" (the first two letters of ΙΗΣΟΥΣ, Jesus) = 18, and T (cross-shape) = 300, thus IHT = 318. This is a creative patristic reading, not a Hebrew interpretation.
- The plain reading: 318 is a plausible number for the fighting force of a wealthy pastoralist chieftain with a large household. It is large enough to be militarily effective in a night raid (v. 15) but not so large as to be implausible for a single extended household.

**וַיָּרֶק ("he mustered/drew out"):** The verb **רִיק** (Hiphil) means literally "to pour out, empty out" — used of drawing a sword from a scabbard (Ps. 35:3; Ezek. 28:7). Here it means "to draw out" his men, "to deploy" or "marshal" them. The EMET renders "mustered" as the closest English military equivalent.

---

### On 14:15 — The Night Attack: Military Tactics

**וַיֵּחָלֵק עֲלֵיהֶם לַיְלָה** — "And he divided against them by night."

The **night attack with divided forces** is a classic guerrilla-warfare tactic: Abram divides his 318 men (and presumably his Amorite allies — Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre, v. 24) into multiple units and attacks simultaneously from different directions under cover of darkness. This creates confusion in the enemy camp and maximizes the shock effect of a smaller force against a larger one.

**Hobah north of Damascus:** The enemy is pursued from the Valley of Siddim (Dead Sea area) as far as **Hobah to the left [north] of Damascus** — a distance of approximately 300+ kilometers. The phrase **מִשְּׂמֹאל לְדַמָּשֶׂק** ("to the left of Damascus") uses the orientation system where one faces east: the north is then to the left. "Left of Damascus" = north of Damascus.

---

### On 14:18–20 — Melchizedek: The Most Theologically Significant Passage

**וּמַלְכִּי-צֶדֶק מֶלֶךְ שָׁלֵם הוֹצִיא לֶחֶם וָיָיִן וְהוּא כֹהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן** — "And Melchizedek king of Salem brought out bread and wine — and he was a priest to God Most High."

This eight-verse episode (vv. 17-20 in relation to the overall narrative, vv. 18-20 for Melchizedek specifically) is arguably the most theologically potent and structurally anomalous passage in the entire Pentateuch. It appears without introduction, explanation, or genealogy. Melchizedek materializes, blesses, and disappears — and will not be named again in the Hebrew Bible until Psalm 110:4.

**מַלְכִּי-צֶדֶק (Melchizedek):** The name is a compound: **מַלְכִּי** ("my king" or "king of") + **צֶדֶק** ("righteousness/justice"). Thus "my king is righteousness" or "king of righteousness." The name may be a throne-name or sacral title rather than (or in addition to) a personal name.

**מֶלֶךְ שָׁלֵם (king of Salem):** שָׁלֵם (Shalem) is widely identified with Jerusalem (יְרוּשָׁלַיִם). The identification is made explicit in Psalm 76:2: "In Salem also is his tabernacle, and his dwelling place in Zion." The name **שָׁלֵם** shares the root of **שָׁלוֹם** (shalom, "peace/wholeness"). Thus: king of peace, ruling the city of peace. The Melchizedek episode maps Jerusalem — the city that will become David's capital and YHWH's chosen dwelling — back into the Abrahamic era as a pre-Israelite sacred site with its own priest of El Elyon.

**כֹהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן ("priest to God Most High"):** Melchizedek holds a dual office:
1. **King (מֶלֶךְ):** political-military ruler
2. **Priest (כֹהֵן):** cultic officiant, mediator of the divine

This combination of king and priest in a single person is extraordinary in the biblical world — Israelite law strictly separates the two offices (the disaster of King Uzziah in 2 Chronicles 26:16-21 illustrates what happens when a king usurps priestly function). Melchizedek represents a pre-Mosaic, pre-Levitical order of priesthood that combines both offices.

**אֵל עֶלְיוֹן (El Elyon, "God Most High"):** The divine title uses two names/epithets:
- **אֵל** (El): the generic Semitic word for "god/God," used across Canaanite and Hebrew religion
- **עֶלְיוֹן** (Elyon, "Most High"): a superlative divine title, "the Highest One"

El Elyon appears as a divine name in Ugaritic texts and throughout the Psalms (Ps. 7:17; 9:2; 21:7; 46:4; 47:2; 57:2; etc.). It is a name used in Canaanite religion for the head of the divine assembly — and Melchizedek, a Canaanite-sphere priest, uses it for the God he serves. Abram immediately adopts this same title and equates it with **יְהוָה** (v. 22): **אֶל-יְהוָה אֵל עֶלְיוֹן** — "to the LORD God Most High." Abram's identification of El Elyon with YHWH is a theological declaration: the god Melchizedek worships is the same as Abram's covenant God. The Most High who created heaven and earth is YHWH.

**The bread and wine:** Melchizedek **"brought out" (הוֹצִיא)** bread and wine to Abram. The nature of this action has generated enormous interpretive discussion:
- A hospitality meal (victuals for Abram and his returning men after the campaign)
- A cultic meal offered to Abram as part of the blessing ceremony
- Both simultaneously

Christian tradition (from the Epistle to the Hebrews and developed patristically) sees a typological prefiguration of the Eucharist in this bread and wine. The EMET translates without endorsing or rejecting this typology; the Hebrew simply states: bread and wine were brought out.

---

### On 14:19 — קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ: "Possessor" or "Creator" of Heaven and Earth?

**קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ** — "possessor of heaven and earth."

The Hebrew **קֹנֵה** is the Qal active participle of **קָנָה**, which has two semantic ranges in Biblical Hebrew:

1. **"To acquire, purchase, possess"** — the standard meaning (Gen. 4:1: "I have acquired [קָנִיתִי] a man"; Gen. 25:10: "the field that Abraham had purchased [קָנָה]"). In this sense: El Elyon is the *possessor*, the one who *owns* heaven and earth.

2. **"To create"** — a secondary but attested meaning, especially in archaic poetry (Deut. 32:6: "Is he not your father who *created* you [קָנֶךָ]?"; Prov. 8:22: "The LORD *created* [קָנָנִי] me at the beginning of his work"). In this sense: El Elyon is the *creator*, the one who *made* heaven and earth.

**Both meanings are exegetically viable and ancient:**
- The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ὃς ἔκτισεν τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν — "who **created** heaven and earth" — choosing the second meaning definitively.
- The Vulgate renders *possessori caeli et terrae* — "possessor of heaven and earth" — choosing the first meaning.
- Modern translations are divided: ESV, NASB, and KJV: "possessor"; NRSV, NJB: "maker/creator."

**The theological weight of the choice is significant:**
- "Possessor" emphasizes sovereignty and ownership: El Elyon holds title to the entire cosmos.
- "Creator" emphasizes ontological priority: El Elyon made heaven and earth ex nihilo.

**The EMET renders "possessor"** as the lexically more common meaning of קָנָה and the one that most directly parallels the legal/commercial domain of the root. However, the translation note marks the ambiguity: in this context, possession of heaven and earth implies creation — one does not possess the cosmos as a secondary acquisition; the only way to possess all things is to have made them. "Possessor" may carry creatorial implications.

The phrase **קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ** recurs verbatim in v. 22 in Abram's oath — the repetition is structurally deliberate, linking Melchizedek's blessing (v. 19) to Abram's oath (v. 22) through identical language. Abram appropriates Melchizedek's theological vocabulary into his declaration to the king of Sodom.

---

### On 14:20 — The Tithe: Who Gives to Whom?

**וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ מַעֲשֵׂר מִכֹּל** — "and he gave him a tithe of all."

The grammatical subject of **וַיִּתֶּן** ("and he gave") is ambiguous — Hebrew does not mark it beyond the implicit pronoun in the verb form. The two possible readings:

1. **Abram gave Melchizedek a tithe of all the spoil** — the most natural reading given the context (Abram is receiving the blessing; a tithe given to a priest as acknowledgment is the expected response). This is the reading adopted by the Letter to the Hebrews (7:2, 4): "Abram gave him a tenth of everything." It is also the reading of virtually all ancient translations (LXX, Vulgate, Targums) and most modern translations.

2. **Melchizedek gave Abram a tithe of all** — a minority reading. This is awkward given that Abram was the one returning with spoil.

**The EMET renders the ambiguous pronoun without resolution**: "and he gave him a tithe of all" — retaining the exact grammatical structure of the Hebrew. Context strongly implies Abram → Melchizedek direction, but the Hebrew is technically ambiguous, and EMET does not add clarity that the text withholds.

**מַעֲשֵׂר (ma'aser, "tithe"):** This is one of the earliest references to tithing in the biblical text — and it pre-dates the Mosaic law. Abram gives a tenth (if he is the subject) without any legal obligation to do so; it is a spontaneous act of recognition toward the priest of El Elyon. The New Testament (Hebrews 7) will use precisely this priority — that the Abrahamic tithe to Melchizedek pre-dates the Levitical system — as evidence for the superiority of the Melchizedekian order of priesthood over the Aaronic-Levitical order.

---

### On 14:22 — יְהוָה אֵל עֶלְיוֹן: The Identification of YHWH with El Elyon

**הֲרִמֹתִי יָדִי אֶל-יְהוָה אֵל עֶלְיוֹן קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ** — "I lift up my hand to the LORD God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth."

This verse is the theological lynchpin of Genesis 14. Abram takes Melchizedek's title for the deity — **אֵל עֶלְיוֹן קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ** ("God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth") — and prefixes it with **יְהוָה** (the LORD), explicitly equating the two:

**The LORD = God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth.**

Melchizedek worships El Elyon. Abram swears by YHWH El Elyon. The identification is Abram's own theological move: he recognizes that Melchizedek's God and his covenant God are the same. This is a remarkable claim: it asserts that a Canaanite priest of pre-Israelite Jerusalem was already worshipping the same deity as the covenant God of Abram — under a different name but with accurate theological understanding (creator/possessor of heaven and earth).

**הֲרִמֹתִי יָד ("I lift up my hand"):** The gesture of oath-taking — raising the hand toward heaven in an oath sworn before God. The perfect tense here may be an "oath perfect" (a performative use: "I hereby lift up my hand" = "I swear"). The oath formula affirms that Abram's refusal to take spoil is not mere generosity or political calculation but a covenant commitment to YHWH alone as his source of wealth and blessing.

**LXX 14:22 — A textual variant:** The LXX (Vaticanus) does not include the Tetragrammaton at this point — it reads simply ἐκτενῶ τὴν χεῖρά μου πρὸς τὸν θεὸν τὸν ὕψιστον ("I will stretch out my hand to God Most High") without the equivalent of יְהוָה. This is a significant LXX divergence: the MT explicitly combines YHWH with El Elyon; the LXX uses only "God Most High." The MT reading is supported by the Samaritan Pentateuch and appears to be original; the LXX may reflect a manuscript tradition (or translational choice) that found the explicit YHWH here premature or problematic in Abram's speech to a Canaanite king. The EMET follows the MT: "the LORD God Most High."

---

### On 14:23-24 — The Thread and the Sandal-Thong: The Oath of Purity

**אִם מֵחוּט וְעַד שְׂרוֹךְ-נַעַל** — "from a thread to a sandal-thong."

This phrase is a **merism** — two extreme, opposite, or minimal items standing for the totality between them. A thread (the smallest, lightest possible item) and a sandal-thong (a utilitarian fastener, the most worthless practical item): together they represent *anything whatsoever*, from the most trivial to the least valuable. The merism is emphatic: Abram will not take *anything* from the king of Sodom — not the most trivial item, not the most negligible scrap.

The **ground of refusal** is explicit: "so that you will not say, I have made Abram rich." Abram refuses on theological grounds — his wealth comes from YHWH alone (cf. the promise of 12:2; the enrichment in Egypt, 12:16). To accept even a thread from the king of Sodom would compromise the testimony that YHWH is the source of Abram's blessing. Abram protects the reputation of his covenant relationship: no one will be able to claim credit for making him rich except YHWH.

**The exception of the Amorite allies (v. 24):** Abram's refusal is for himself alone — he explicitly exempts his Amorite partners (Aner, Eshcol, Mamre) from the oath. They are not bound by his covenant with YHWH; they fought for legitimate payment and shall receive their share. This is an act of scrupulous fairness: Abram does not impose his personal theological commitments on others who do not share them.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 14

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 14:1 | גּוֹיִם ("nations/Goiim") | ἐθνῶν ("of nations") | LXX transliterates the sense; MT preserves the proper name Goiim |
| 14:13 | הָעִבְרִי ("the Hebrew") | τῷ περάτῃ ("the crosser/one from beyond") | LXX translates etymologically ("one who crosses over") rather than transliterating; reflects the "crossing over" etymology |
| 14:14 | חֲנִיכָיו ("his trained men") | τοὺς ἰδίους οἰκογενεῖς ("his own home-born") | LXX renders יְלִידֵי בֵיתוֹ element; the hapax חֲנִיכִים not distinctly rendered |
| 14:18 | לֶחֶם וָיָיִן ("bread and wine") | ἄρτους καὶ οἶνον ("breads and wine") | LXX uses plural ἄρτους for "breads"; MT has singular לֶחֶם (collective) |
| 14:19 | קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ ("possessor of heaven and earth") | ὃς ἔκτισεν τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν ("who created heaven and earth") | Significant: LXX chooses "creator" for קֹנֵה; MT is ambiguous; EMET follows MT with "possessor" |
| 14:20 | וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ מַעֲשֵׂר ("and he gave him a tithe") | καὶ ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ δεκάτην ("and he gave him a tenth") | LXX agrees; subject ambiguity preserved in both |
| 14:22 | יְהוָה אֵל עֶלְיוֹן ("the LORD God Most High") | τὸν θεὸν τὸν ὕψιστον ("God Most High") — no κύριος | Major variant: LXX lacks יְהוָה; MT has the full compound name YHWH El Elyon |
| 14:23 | שְׂרוֹךְ-נַעַל ("sandal-thong") | σφαιρωτῆρα ὑποδήματος ("sandal-buckle/clasp") | LXX renders differently; MT's שְׂרוֹךְ = strap/lace/thong |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls, Samaritan Pentateuch, and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** The **Genesis Apocryphon (1QapGen)**, cols. XXI-XXII, provides an extended Aramaic retelling of Genesis 14. The Apocryphon expands dramatically: it gives geographic details of the campaign route, elaborates Abram's pursuit of the four kings, and expands the Melchizedek encounter. Textually, however, it confirms the substance of the MT rather than offering variants. The **11QMelchizedek scroll (11Q13)** is a major Dead Sea document that interprets Melchizedek eschatologically as a heavenly figure who executes divine judgment — but this is a midrashic/pesher interpretation, not a Genesis 14 text variant.

**Samaritan Pentateuch:** The SP agrees with the MT throughout Genesis 14 without significant variants.

**Psalm 110:4 — The Melchizedek Trajectory:** **לְעוֹלָם עַל-דִּבְרָתִי מַלְכִּי-צֶדֶק** — "You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek." Psalm 110 makes Melchizedek the paradigm of an eternal, non-Levitical priesthood associated with the Davidic king. This single verse transforms the Genesis 14 episode into a foundational text for a theory of royal-priestly combination that bypasses the Mosaic-Levitical system entirely.

**New Testament (Hebrews 5-7):** The Letter to the Hebrews develops the Melchizedek typology exhaustively, arguing that Jesus Christ is the fulfillment of the Melchizedekian order of priesthood — eternal (Heb. 7:3: "without father, without mother, without genealogy, having neither beginning of days nor end of life"), superior to the Levitical order (Abram tithed to Melchizedek, therefore Levi — still in Abram's loins — paid tithe to Melchizedek, Heb. 7:9-10), and combining king and priest in one person. The EMET renders Genesis 14 without theological overlay; the NT trajectory is noted for the reader's orientation but does not govern the translation.

**Targum Onkelos:** Renders the passage closely following the MT. At 14:13, Onkelos renders **הָעִבְרִי** as "the Hebrew" (transliterating). At 14:19-20, **קֹנֵה** is rendered in a sense closer to "possessor/owner" (following MT rather than LXX's "creator").

**Josephus (Antiquities 1.10.2):** Josephus describes Melchizedek as a Canaanite chief who was righteous and a priest, identifies Salem with Jerusalem, and says Melchizedek provided Abram's army with abundance of all things. He reflects the historical reading of Genesis 14 as a military episode with diplomatic aftermath.
